How to Choose a Standing Desk

Standing desks have moved from novelty to standard equipment in the modern home office, but the market is crowded and the price range is enormous. This guide walks through the decisions that actually matter: frame stability, height range, motor quality, and desktop material. Skip the marketing noise and focus on how the desk will feel after two years of daily use, because that is where cheap frames reveal themselves with wobble, motor whine, and stripped mounting threads.

Frame stability comes first

A desk that shakes when you type is a desk you will stop raising. Look for dual-motor frames with crossbars or oversized columns, and check the wobble at maximum height in person if you can. Reviews that measure lateral deflection are worth more than any spec sheet. Most budget frames are perfectly stable at sitting height and noticeably springy above 120 centimeters, which matters if you are tall or keep a monitor arm clamped to the rear edge of the desktop.

Height range and your body

Measure your elbow height when standing in the shoes you actually wear at home. The desk should reach at least that high, and ideally a few centimeters higher so you are not locked into a single posture. Shorter users should check the minimum height too, since many frames bottom out around 72 centimeters, which is too tall for comfortable seated typing without a keyboard tray. A good frame disappears into your routine; a bad one becomes a coat rack within a month, and no amount of reminder apps will change that.
